QF.id: VII Introduction to Securities Broking Operations_16

question.text: An Immediate or Cancel (IOC) order is best described as:

answer.text: An order that must be executed immediately; any unmatched portion is cancelled instantly
An order that remains in the system until cancelled by the trader
An order that is valid for the entire trading day
An order that gets activated only when a trigger price is reached

explanation.text: **The Logic:** IOC order must execute immediately upon release into the market. Partial matching is possible — if only part of the order finds a counter, that part trades and the remainder is cancelled instantly. **The Trap:** A Day order stays in the system until end of day. GTC/GTD orders persist across days (currently not available per SEBI directives). IOC is the most time-restrictive order type. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series VIII covers order types in derivative markets. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Order time conditions: IOC = instant (partial ok, rest cancelled). Day = valid till end of day. GTC/GTD = currently not available. COL (Cancel on Logout) = cancelled when user logs out. Most retail orders are Day orders; IOC is popular for algorithmic trading.
